content: The first mile of a product — particularly the first 30 seconds — determines whether users continue or abandon. In this window, users behave according to three predictable traits: they are lazy (unwilling to invest effort to understand), vain (concerned about how they appear to others), and selfish (demanding immediate personal return before committing). Belsky argues that more than 30% of total product energy should be allocated to this narrow window, because getting it wrong makes everything else irrelevant.
implication: Teams should audit where their design and engineering investment actually goes. If less than a third of effort is going to first-run experience and onboarding, the allocation is almost certainly wrong — no matter how sophisticated the core product is.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book) / First Round Review: 'How to Shape Remarkable Products in the Messy Middle'

content: Belsky frames the first-mile problem through a simple but powerful hierarchy: doing something proactively for the user beats showing them how to do it, which in turn beats explaining why they should. Most products over-invest in explanation — onboarding tutorials, tooltip walkthroughs, and help documentation — while under-investing in just doing the work on behalf of the user so value is felt immediately without any cognitive load.
implication: Before writing another line of onboarding copy or adding a product tour, ask whether the feature could simply be pre-configured, pre-populated, or pre-executed for the user. The ideal onboarding experience is one where the user never needs to be taught anything.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: The product lifecycle follows a predictable and self-destructive arc: simplicity attracts early users, success incentivizes adding features, feature accumulation creates complexity, and complexity drives customers toward newer, simpler competitors. Belsky identifies this as one of the most reliable failure patterns in product development — not a result of bad intent but of unchecked incremental decisions that each seem reasonable in isolation.
implication: Every feature addition should be evaluated not just on its own merits but on what it does to the overall simplicity of the product. The discipline to say no — or to remove existing features — is as important a product skill as the ability to conceive new ones.
source_ref: First Round Review: 'How to Shape Remarkable Products in the Messy Middle of Building Startups'

content: Belsky argues that the first hook in a product experience must connect a user's immediate, short-term self-interest to a longer-term promise. Users are not going to project forward and imagine future benefits — they need to feel value in the moment, anchored to something they already care about. The hook is not a marketing message; it is a product design problem that has to be solved in the actual experience.
implication: Map out what a new user wants in the first 30 seconds — not what your product does, but what that specific user cares about right now. Design the first interaction around delivering precisely that, and let the broader value proposition emerge from experience rather than explanation.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Radical simplification is not a design preference — it is a strategic discipline. Belsky contends that the willingness to remove features, reduce options, and narrow scope is what separates enduring products from ones that collapse under their own complexity. The hardest product decisions are not what to build but what to cut, especially when the thing being cut was built with care and has existing users.
implication: Build a recurring practice of subtraction into your product process — not just sprint reviews for what to add next, but deliberate sessions focused on what to remove. The products that compound in value over time are almost always the ones that got more focused, not more comprehensive.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky contends that most products fail not because they lack features but because they fail to make users feel capable and successful immediately. The vanity dimension of the lazy-vain-selfish framework is underappreciated: users care deeply about how using a product makes them look to others, and a product that makes someone feel stupid or incompetent in its first moments will be abandoned regardless of its power.
implication: Design the first experience to make users feel competent and even impressive. Early wins that users can share or reference — output they're proud of — are among the most effective retention and acquisition mechanisms available, and they're primarily a product design decision, not a marketing one.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book) / First Round Review: 'How to Shape Remarkable Products in the Messy Middle of Building Startups'

content: Belsky warns against what he calls the idea generation trap — the tendency for creative and product teams to constantly produce new ideas rather than executing and refining existing ones. The seduction of new ideas feels like productivity but is often a form of avoidance. The real skill is not generating ideas but developing the organizational machinery to execute the ones already in hand.
implication: Evaluate whether your team's energy is concentrated on executing the current roadmap or dispersed across exciting new directions. Idea generation should be rationed; execution bandwidth is the actual constraint in most product organizations.
source_ref: Scott Belsky: How To Avoid The Idea Generation Trap

content: Belsky argues that a product's core differentiator should never be subject to MVP-style corner-cutting. The minimum viable product framework is valuable for peripheral features and unproven hypotheses, but applying it to the thing that makes you different communicates low conviction to the market and trains early adopters to expect mediocrity. If something is your reason for existing, it deserves disproportionate craft and investment from day one.
implication: Be explicit about what is and is not eligible for MVP treatment on your roadmap. Peripheral features, integrations, and supporting workflows can be rough — but the core value proposition should launch with the highest craft your team can produce, even if that means launching later or smaller.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Belsky observes that the best product teams ground their decisions in simple, durable convictions about human behavior rather than in feature requests or competitive analysis. These convictions — about how users actually behave, what they actually want, and what friction they will never tolerate — act as a north star that prevents the drift toward complexity that kills most products over time.
implication: Before building, articulate two or three behavioral convictions about your users that your entire product strategy will honor. These are not personas or use cases — they are non-negotiable truths about human nature that your product will always serve, regardless of what the competitive landscape demands.
source_ref: First Round Review: 'How to Shape Remarkable Products in the Messy Middle of Building Startups'

content: The selfishness dimension of new user behavior — the expectation of immediate personal return before any investment — means that onboarding cannot ask for effort before delivering value. Any product that requires users to upload data, configure settings, invite colleagues, or complete a profile before experiencing a benefit will lose a significant portion of its potential user base in that gap. The value must precede the ask.
implication: Audit every step in your onboarding flow for whether it extracts effort before delivering value. Every ask that precedes a tangible payoff is a dropout risk. The sequence should be: value first, investment second — always.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book) / Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky argues that product teams consistently underestimate the emotional and psychological state of a brand-new user. That person arrives with no context, limited patience, competing distractions, and deep skepticism about whether this product is worth their time. Designing for this person — rather than for an informed, motivated user who already understands the value proposition — is the fundamental discipline of first-mile product design.
implication: Regularly stress-test your product with genuinely cold users who have zero context or prior exposure. The gap between how your team experiences the product and how a cold user experiences it is the first-mile problem made visible — and most teams are shocked by how wide that gap is.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky identifies the tendency to keep adding features as one of the most dangerous organizational instincts in product companies. Each new feature feels like progress and satisfies a real user request, but the cumulative effect is a product that becomes harder to explain, harder to onboard, and harder to love. The discipline to say no to features — including features users have explicitly requested — is a form of product leadership that is rare and essential.
implication: Create deliberate forcing functions that make feature addition harder, not easier. Require every new feature to retire an existing one. Make the default answer to feature requests 'no' and require a high burden of proof for 'yes.' The best product leaders are known as much for what they killed as for what they shipped.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Belsky's concept of the first mile extends beyond onboarding screens to encompass the entire first episode of value a user has with a product — from how they discover it, to their first interaction, to the first time they get a result they care about. Each step in this journey is a potential dropout point, and the cumulative drop-off across these steps means that a far smaller percentage of people who encounter a product ever experience what makes it truly great.
implication: Map the full first-mile funnel from discovery to first meaningful outcome, and measure the dropout rate at each step. Most teams know their signup conversion but not their 'first value' conversion. The gap between those two numbers is the first-mile problem quantified.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky argues that the product teams doing the most important work are often the ones working on the least glamorous problems — the empty states, the error messages, the loading screens, the edge cases of onboarding. These moments are where the majority of users form their lasting impression of a product, yet they receive a fraction of the design and engineering attention that goes to headline features. Craft in the margins is what separates good products from great ones.
implication: Explicitly allocate design resources to edge cases and negative-state experiences: empty states when there's no data, error recovery flows, the experience of a user who got confused and is trying again. These moments disproportionately determine retention and word-of-mouth relative to the attention they typically receive.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Belsky draws a direct line between product simplicity and the ability to tell a compelling story about a product. Products that have drifted into complexity become impossible to explain simply, which makes them harder to sell, harder to market, and harder for existing users to evangelize to others. Simplicity is not just a UX virtue — it is a business development and growth asset that compounds over time.
implication: Use the one-sentence product explanation as a diagnostic tool. If your team cannot agree on a single clear sentence that describes what your product does and for whom, the product itself has likely accumulated too much complexity. Simplifying the product often makes the story clear, not the reverse.
source_ref: Building Adobe & the Future of Design with Scott Belsky

content: Belsky contends that the most important product decisions are not made in roadmap planning sessions or design reviews but in the daily micro-decisions about what to prioritize, what to defer, and what to cut. These decisions accumulate over months and years to define what a product actually is — not what the strategy deck says it is. The gap between intended product direction and actual product direction is almost always explained by these unchecked micro-decisions.
implication: Establish clear, shared product principles that can guide micro-decisions without requiring a meeting. When every small decision requires escalation or debate, the product drifts — not dramatically but incrementally, in ways that are only visible in retrospect. Principles make the strategy executable at the level of daily judgment calls.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Belsky observes that AI is fundamentally changing the first-mile problem by enabling products to be deeply personalized before the user has ever done anything. An AI-powered product can infer context, adapt its interface, and pre-configure its experience based on signals that previously required extensive user input. This shifts the first-mile opportunity from 'how do we teach the user quickly' to 'how do we already know what the user needs.'
implication: Teams building with AI should reimagine onboarding from scratch rather than applying AI as a layer on top of existing flows. The question is no longer how to walk users through setup — it's how to eliminate setup entirely by letting the product infer and adapt from the first interaction.
source_ref: Scott Belsky on AI and the Return of a Hyper-Personalized World

content: Belsky argues that product teams should be deeply suspicious of their own engagement metrics as proxies for genuine user value. High engagement can reflect compulsion, confusion, or inefficiency as easily as it reflects delight. The question is not whether users are spending time in the product but whether they are achieving outcomes they care about and whether the product is making them feel capable and successful.
implication: Add outcome-based metrics alongside engagement metrics in your product dashboards. Measure whether users achieved what they came to do, not just whether they spent time doing it. The products that earn long-term loyalty are the ones that respect users' time by making them faster and more capable, not the ones that maximize time-on-site.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky argues that taste is itself a product design input, not just an aesthetic preference. The decisions that define a product's character — what to include, how to sequence interactions, what friction to preserve versus eliminate, what to make prominent versus buried — are acts of editorial judgment that require developed taste. Teams that lack taste produce products that are technically functional but emotionally inert.
implication: Invest in the taste development of your product team the same way you invest in their technical skills. This means exposing them to great products across categories, building a shared vocabulary for what makes experiences feel right, and creating a culture where aesthetic judgment is treated as a legitimate form of product expertise.
source_ref: Where Scott Belsky Got His Good Taste

content: Belsky makes the case that the best product leaders are defined less by what they build and more by what they protect. Protecting simplicity against organizational pressure to add features, protecting the first-mile experience against the pull of deeper-funnel optimization, and protecting the core differentiator against the temptation to expand scope — these acts of protection are where product leadership is most acutely tested and most consequentially exercised.
implication: Reframe the product leader role as guardian as much as builder. Actively identify the two or three things about your product that must never be compromised, and create organizational awareness around protecting them. The things most worth protecting are usually the things under the most pressure from internal stakeholders.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Belsky points out that the creative tools category — which he has studied and worked within deeply throughout his career at Adobe — faces a particular version of the first-mile problem: creative tools are often powerful but intimidating, and the gap between what the tool can do and what a new user can do with it in the first session is enormous. Bridging that gap is not just a product nicety but a business imperative, because users who don't create something they're proud of in the first session almost never return.
implication: For any product where output quality matters to the user, design the first-session experience around guaranteeing a high-quality output — even if that means heavily constraining the user's choices in that first session. Let them produce something they're proud to share before you expose them to the full power of the tool.
source_ref: Building Adobe & the Future of Design with Scott Belsky

content: Belsky argues that product teams often confuse user feedback with product direction. Users can accurately report what frustrates them but rarely can they articulate what would genuinely delight them — because the most breakthrough product experiences are ones users could not have imagined asking for. The discipline is to listen carefully to user pain while maintaining independent conviction about the solution, which may look nothing like what users think they want.
implication: Use user research to understand pain points deeply and rigorously, but don't let it define the solution space. The team's job is to invent solutions to real problems, not to implement the solutions users describe. The best product outcomes come from marrying rigorous user empathy with independent creative conviction.
source_ref: The Messy Middle (book)

content: Belsky notes that the prompt interface — the blank text box that dominated early generative AI products — is itself a first-mile failure for most users. Most people don't know what to ask for, feel exposed by a blank page, and produce mediocre results without knowing how to improve them. The next generation of AI creative tools will replace the prompt box with more intuitive, tactile, direct-manipulation interfaces that guide users to great outcomes without requiring them to master prompt engineering.
implication: If you're building AI-powered products, treat the prompt box as a transitional interface and invest in discovering what replaces it for your specific user. The teams who solve the first-mile problem for AI tools — making them feel as intuitive as the best non-AI products — will capture the mainstream market that current AI tools consistently fail to convert.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response
